title: Thinking through central banks' next moves
gist: Capital Economics classifies major central banks into four groups post-Middle East energy shock: defensive hikes no longer needed (Indonesia, Pakistan, Turkey), cuts incoming (Brazil, Russia, Hungary, Israel), more work to do (Japan, Australia, Korea), and hiking-then-cutting (ECB, Bank of England)—with the Fed sitting closest to Group 3, leading Capital Economics to forecast two rate hikes to 4.00–4.25%, calling markets too dovish.

insights.claim: Capital Economics divides major central banks into four post-energy-shock groups: (1) EM defensive hikes no longer needed (Indonesia, Pakistan, Turkey), (2) cuts incoming (Brazil, Russia, Colombia, Hungary, Israel), (3) more tightening needed (Japan, Australia, Korea, Czechia, Chile), and (4) hiking-then-cutting (ECB, Bank of England, potentially ECB despite recent hike).
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: This taxonomy provides a systematic cross-market framework for positioning in rate-sensitive assets; Group 3 countries offer the clearest case for short-duration positioning, while Group 2 signals rate-sensitive long opportunities.
insights.locator: it is useful to divide central banks into four groups

insights.claim: Capital Economics expects the Bank of Japan to raise rates to around 2% by the end of next year—among the clearest examples of Group 3 (more work to do) central banks where labour markets are tight, policy settings remain insufficiently restrictive, and inflation pressures are building.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: BoJ rate path toward 2% is a sustained JPY tailwind and a risk for long-duration Japanese government bond holders; investors should watch BoJ's pace closely as a potential trigger for global duration repricing.
insights.quote: we continue to expect rates to rise to around 2% by the end of next year
insights.locator: The most important example is the Bank of Japan

insights.claim: Capital Economics expects ECB insurance hikes this year to be unwound in 2027—the energy shock is smaller than 2022, labour markets less tight, policy less accommodative, and inflation expectations well-anchored with little evidence of second-round effects; market pricing for 2027 ECB rates looks too high.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: European rate markets are pricing in too much tightening persistence into 2027—a potential long opportunity in 2-year bunds/OATs if the ECB pivots to cuts as Capital Economics expects.
insights.quote: any insurance hikes this year are likely to be unwound in 2027
insights.locator: The case for a significant and sustained tightening

themes.theme: Fed: Closest to Group 3, Hawkish Lean

insights.claim: Capital Economics forecasts two Fed rate hikes in 2026 to 4.00–4.25%, citing: core PCE at 3.3% y/y (likely to edge higher), US GDP Nowcast of ~3% Q2 annualised, tight labour market, highly expansionary fiscal policy, and AI not yet generating a disinflationary impulse—and new Chair Kevin Warsh stated 'we've got some work to do on the price stability point.'
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Capital Economics is firmly in the 'markets too dovish on Fed' camp; the 4.00–4.25% target range forecast requires duration caution on US Treasuries and suggests dollar strength persistence.
insights.quote: we now expect two rate hikes this year, to take the fed funds target range to 4.00-4.25%
insights.locator: we now expect two rate hikes this year

insights.claim: Capital Economics explicitly notes that AI adoption does not appear to be generating a meaningful disinflationary impulse and 'may, at the margin, actually be contributing to inflation pressures'—a contrarian view on AI deflation narratives.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: The consensus that AI is disinflationary is not yet visible in US data; investors building inflation-hedge positions on the assumption that AI deflation is imminent may be early.
insights.quote: The adoption of AI does not yet appear to be generating a meaningful disinflationary impulse and may, at the margin, actually be contributing to inflation pressures.
insights.locator: The adoption of AI does not yet appear

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh scaled back forward guidance at his first meeting as promised, abstained from the dot plot, but said 'we've got some work to do on the price stability point'—putting him in an awkward position having been appointed with a mandate to lower rates while facing an economy that requires tighter policy.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Warsh's hawkish rhetoric at his first meeting establishes a higher hurdle for early Fed easing; markets that were pricing cuts are being forced to reprice toward Warsh's mandate-first framing.
insights.quote: 'we've got some work to do on the price stability point'
insights.locator: Having been appointed with a mandate to lower interest rates
